abstract: Last year I wrote the JOB ARCDI editorial about my excitement over the artificial intelligence (AI) tool ChatGPT (Dasborough, 2023). To my great surprise, some scholars read the editorial, incorporating and citing it in their own work! Back when that editorial was first published, ChatGPT was relatively new in our field and not many scholars in OB were using it. However, today, it is a very different story. Many OB scholars are using ChatGPT regularly to assist them with fulfilling their research, teaching, and service commitments. The popularity of ChatGPT has grown extremely quickly (faster than Instagram and TikToc!; Berg et al., 2023), and there has even been a ChatGPT review article published (Ayinde et al., 2023). ChatGPT is not the only AI tool that scholars have available to use. Today, a plethora of AI tools now help make scholars live a little easier. As I reviewed the new submissions to the JOB ARCDI over the last few weeks, something that stood out to me was how many of the papers mentioned their use of AI. I had not heard of many of these AI tools before, and I had to look them up online to learn about their capabilities. While some of these AI tools are newly developed, others have been around for a while and have been used frequently in other fields of study (e.g., the tool “Rayyan”; see Ouzzani et al., 2016). It may be that AI tools provide unique forms of assistance for different parts of the review process. For example, DistillerSR primarily helps with screening articles and extracting data from them. EPPI-Reviewer helps managing references, screening articles, coding, and research synthesis. Rayyan, JBI SUMARI, and Covidence suggest which articles to include in a review (data screening) and help produce the review, as well as facilitate cooperation with multiple co-authors conducting reviews. 1 Some AI tools, such as ChatGPT, can be used to brainstorm ideas for papers, create paper outlines, summarize, and paraphrase text, and provide feedback on drafts. Reporting this type of writing assistance may be more challenging than reporting assistance with a specific task such as screening articles; however, it can be done (see Appendix A for an illustration). The second reason that I am pleased to see the reporting of AI tools in JOB ARCDI manuscripts is because it facilitates learning. I love to learn about new tools available, and I feel joy in knowing that this information is also being shared with our JOB audience (see Gagné et al., 2019). If some scholars are benefitting from AI tools, many of which are freely available, then it is leveling the playing field if these tools are then shared with others. When scholars report their selected “tools of the trade,” it helps advance our science and we all benefit from it as scholars. 2 There are eight articles in this issue of ARCDI, each of which may or may not have benefitted from the use of AI tools. As usual, these articles cover a wide range of topics that will hopefully keep the JOB audience engaged and motivated to start new research projects of their own. The first three articles in this issue build on the theme of constant change in the workplace. They provide insights into the intricate relationships between technological advancements, new forms of employment, and the creative processes that drive organizational innovation. First, Bankins and colleagues present a multilevel review of artificial intelligence in organizations, which sheds light on the transformative impact of AI on organizational structures, processes, and human-AI dynamics. This review not only delves into the implications for future research on AI at work but also provides managers with guidance on prioritizing employee well-being while embracing the use of AI. The emergence of AI platforms and algorithms has enabled the automation of certain tasks, leading to the creation of new gig work opportunities, as well as streamlining the matching process of gig workers and employers. 3 The second paper by Wu and Huang reviews the literature on this specific type of work, gig work. They outline the main themes in the gig work literature, calling for future research in the most pressing areas that need development, and outline practical implications for gig workers, organizations, digital labor platforms, and the broader society. Given the current context of evolving work structures, fostering creativity in the workplace is of great importance. In the third article, Liu and colleagues provide an insightful review of the creativity literature that shows that creativity may result in various outcomes for creative individuals, their coworkers, teams, and organizations. This multilevel examination will be useful to both scholars (especially the table of definitions and the integrative summary figure) and managers who may use this information to make better decisions around managing creativity in the workplace. The next three articles cover topics that may be a little uncomfortable for people to discuss with others in the workplace. Yet, they are also highly relatable for many of us. At times during my own career, I have experienced feeling like an imposter, I have felt self-ambivalence, and I have been perplexed by competitive women acting as “cats,” “bitches,” and “queen bees.” In the first of these articles, Gullifor and colleagues shed light on the imposter phenomenon. Although this is not a new concept and many scholars experience it (Wilkinson, 2020), until now there had not been a comprehensive review of the literature on this topic. In addition to providing a review, the authors develop an integrative framework and advance a new understanding of imposter phenomenon using a trait and state-based approach. The next article by Hoobler, Masterson, and Rogers introduces the construct of “self-ambivalence,” which refers to having a simultaneously oppositional orientation toward oneself (feeling both positive and negative at the same time). This is a work–family problem that—until now—had no name. Their conceptual model begins with “work–family ought events” that come with possibilities and limitations, prompting identity-related self-examination. Their conceptualization of this new construct has implications for self-concept clarity and well-being. Individuals, especially women, may feel such self-ambivalence when they act in a competitive way at work instead of adhering with prescriptive gender norms that promote cooperation (see Hanek et al., 2016). Next, Kark and colleagues provide an interdisciplinary review of the literature on competition between women at work (who may end up being labeled as “cats,” “bitches,” “queen bees,” or “sisters”). They synthesize this body of literature and develop a paradoxical framework of coopetition (competitive–cooperation) in order to guide future research. They also suggest some tools to help managers deal with pressures that come from paradoxical expectations for women at work. Like the previous articles, the final two articles also offer strong conceptual development. Galvin and colleagues propose new theory to answer: how, why, and when do the right or wrong leaders emerge (or fail to emerge)? The authors integrate leadership theories to better understand how to identify and develop effective leaders in the workplace. The final article by Olekalns and Caza reconceptualizes the relationship repair process. Extending the established work on trust repair, the authors unpack two other processes that have been largely ignored in the literature until now: relationship work and sensemaking. Their reconceptualization highlights how narrative foundations can shape the vulnerable elements of a relationships and determine what repair processes will be most effective. This approach to relationship repair will be useful for relationships both inside the workplace and at home. While AI may, or may not, have been used by the authors of these eight ARCDI articles to help write drafts of their work, each article offers a unique critique of the literature and/or creative spins on theory that cannot yet be replicated by AI tools, such as ChatGPT. I hope to continue seeing this human influence on JOB articles in the years to come. As an editor, I see this as being where the real scholarly contribution lies. There is a significant difference between human-generated texts versus AI-generated texts (see Lindebaum & Fleming, 2023). I also fear that I could become disengaged without such unique human insights that both inspire and entertain me. In the past, I have questioned how AI tools such as ChatGPT would be acknowledged by authors as we move into the “ChatGPT Era.” Today, we are beginning to see transparency in the use of AI tools, with an open appreciation for AI being expressed in manuscripts being submitted to JOB. Moving forward, I hope to see this appreciation continue, with our AI assistants that are used at various stages of the literature review process being reported (e.g., in a PRISMA flow diagram). In addition to aiding literature reviews as reported earlier, machine learning and other AI techniques are being used to complement theoretical development (see Leavitt et al., 2021; Lindebaum & Ashraf, 2023). This will need acknowledgement too. I also want scholars to learn about new AI tools (and non-AI tools) after reading JOB ARCDI publications. Not only can we learn about the conceptual content of these papers, but we can also learn about the new tools being used in our field to help develop our own scholarly work. Moving beyond the acknowledgement and appreciation of AI tools in manuscripts, I would also like to extend a call for gratitude to the humans who helped with submissions to JOB ARCDI. I call for submitting authors to report both “what” (the tools) and “who” they collaborated with to produce their scholarly work. In addition to AI assistants, authors should be transparent about the use of human research assistants, doctoral students, editors, proofreaders, and so on. Even if authorship is not warranted, I encourage authors to express their gratitude to others in a written acknowledgment for their supportive efforts (see Locklear et al., 2023). After all, if we can thank the robots, we most certainly should thank all the human members of our “behind-the-scenes academic teams.” I would like to express my gratitude to Beth Campbell who helped with brainstorming ideas for this editorial and to Heath Gregg and Christian Resick for providing feedback and proofreading it prior to publication. In addition, while generative AI was not used in the idea-generating phase or writing of this editorial, I do acknowledge the use of ChatGPT for one specific task. I appreciate ChatGPT for assisting with the title (for full transparency and entertainment purposes, see Appendix A for ChatGPT prompts and alternative title offerings). There is no conflict of interest. This is an editorial (introducing the annual review issue of a journal) that starts with commentary about the new tools of the trade, specifically the use of AI to help with writing review articles. In this commentary, the main theme will be the need to be transparent about the use of tools used (all tools/resources, not just ChatGPT). In addition to openly acknowledging these AI tools, there is also a call for authors to write acknowledgements to other humans who assisted them in the process (doctoral students, research assistants, proofreaders, etc.). This commentary is then followed by a summary of the eight articles in this issue of the journal. The conclusion of this commentary will be about embracing the wide range of new tools available in our work, while being transparent and acknowledging what tools and people helped produce it. “AI in Academia: The Clear Case for Transparency in Authorship” “Acknowledging the Machine: The Role of AI and Transparency in Scholarly Reviews” “Tools of the Transparent Trade: AI's Place in Academic Writing” “From Chatbots to ChatGPT: Unveiling AI's Role in Research Reviews” “The Ghost in the Machine: Crediting AI and Human Contributions in Research” “Transparent Texts: Declaring AI Assistance in Academic Reviews” “AI-thors of Our Own Stories: Transparency in Academic Tools” “Bots in the Bylines: Recognizing Invisible Helpers in Scholarly Work” “Artificial Assistance: The Ethical Imperative for Transparency in AI-Written Reviews” “From Human Hands to AI Lands: Acknowledging All Helpers in Academic Publishing.” Suggested titles that are “More Clever and Witty.” “Cite-Seeing with AI: A Transparent Journey Through Academic Reviews” “Ghostwriters in the Machine: Citing AI Collaborators in Scholarly Work” “The Scholar's New Clothes: Dressing Up Research with AI Transparency” “Bot or Not? Outlining AI's Role in the Review Process” “Write of Passage: Acknowledging AI in the Ivory Tower” “Machine Learning and Human Earning: Credit Where It's AI-Due” “From Deep Learning to Deep Citing: AI and the Ethics of Transparency” “The Acknowledgment Algorithm: AI Contributions Unveiled” “Tool Reveal: Unmasking AI in Academic Articulations” “Compu-credits: Listing AI Among Academic Aides” Suggested titles that also “Capture Related Feelings.” “Assistance with Honest Acknowledgments” “Feeling Scholarly: The Emotional Intelligence of Citing AI” “Gratitude in the Machine: Recognizing AI's Emotional Labor” “Transparent Affections: Loving the AI That Shapes Our Literature” “The Sentimental Scholar: AI and the Etiquette of Academic Acknowledgment” “Robotic Revelations: The Heartfelt Citing of Silicon Colleagues” “Affectionate Algorithms: The Warm Side of Cold Coding” “Passion for Precision: AI's Role in Rigorous Reviews” “Empathy Engines: Acknowledging the AIs Behind Our Academic Emprise.” Final title written by author using inspiration from ChatGPT suggestions: “Ghostwriters in the Machine: Openly Appreciating AI Tools and Humans Who Helped Us.” Dr. Marie Dasborough is a Professor of Management at the University of Miami. She received her Ph.D. in Organizational Behavior from the University of Queensland in Australia. She is the Co-President of the Network of Leadership Scholars and also serves on the board of the Southern Management Association. At the Journal of Organizational Behavior, she is the editor for the Annual Review and Conceptual Development Issue and Point-Counterpoint. Dr. Dasborough's fields of research include leadership, emotions, and teamwork. She has authored articles in outlets such as the Academy of Management Review, Journal of Applied Psychology, Journal of Business Ethics, Journal of Organizational Behavior, The Leadership Quarterly, The Journal of Management, and numerous others. To stimulate research ideas and to facilitate data collection efforts, she has partnered with various organizations, including: Bacardi, NBC Universal Telemundo, Netflix, Barrick Gold Corporation, Boston Consulting Group, FlipKart, McDonnells Law, Joondalup Health Campus, University of Miami Miller School of Medicine, Main Roads Department Australia, and Coconut Grove Bank.

ob.ai_reason: Editorial on ChatGPT and AI tools in OB research.
ob.tldr: 编辑反思AI工具（尤其是ChatGPT）如何迅速普及于组织行为学研究领域。作者指出，当前许多投稿中都提及AI工具的使用，这些工具覆盖了文献筛选（如DistillerSR、Rayyan）、写作辅助等功能。编辑呼吁学者在使用AI时保持透明，既感谢工具的协助，也感谢提供帮助的人类同行。

title: Ghostwriters in the machine: Openly appreciating AI tools and humans who helped us
title_zh: 机器中的幽灵写手：公开致谢 AI 工具与帮助我们的人
